skill.name: TypeScript Expert
skill.description: Expert TypeScript and JavaScript guidance with deep knowledge of type systems, performance optimization, and modern tooling.
skill.summary: Advanced TypeScript assistant for type-level programming, build optimization, monorepo management, and migration strategies.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 64 patterns but manual review confirms most are false positives. The backtick patterns are TypeScript template literal types and markdown code fences, not shell execution. URLs are JSON schema references. One legitimate finding: subprocess.run with shell=True in local diagnostic script uses only hardcoded commands with no user input injection risk. No network activity, credential access, or data exfiltration detected.

medium_findings.title: Subprocess with shell=True
medium_findings.description: Python diagnostic script uses subprocess.run with shell=True which could enable command injection if user input reaches the command parameter. Review confirms all commands are hardcoded strings with no external input, making this safe for local diagnostic use.

low_findings.title: False positive: Template literal types flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer incorrectly flagged TypeScript template literal types (e.g., `${infer T}`) in utility-types.ts as shell backtick execution. These are type-level string manipulation, not command execution.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown code fences flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer incorrectly flagged markdown code fence delimiters (```typescript) in documentation as shell backtick execution. These are documentation formatting, not executable code.

low_findings.title: False positive: JSON schema URL flagged as hardcoded network endpoint
low_findings.description: The $schema reference to json.schemastore.org is a standard TypeScript configuration pattern for IDE autocomplete, not an active network request.

content.user_title: Master TypeScript Development with Expert Guidance
content.value_statement: Struggling with complex TypeScript types, build performance, or migration challenges? Get expert guidance on type-level programming, modern tooling, and best practices from a specialized TypeScript assistant.
actual_capabilities: Analyze TypeScript project configuration and detect issues with tsconfig settings
Implement advanced type patterns including branded types, conditional types, and template literal types
Diagnose and resolve type errors, module resolution issues, and build performance problems
Guide JavaScript to TypeScript migration with incremental strategies
Configure monorepo setups with project references and tool selection (Nx, Turborepo)
Review TypeScript code for type safety, performance, and best practices
limitations: Cannot execute TypeScript compiler or bundler commands directly, only provides diagnostic commands
Type checking requires user to run provided commands in their local environment
Does not have access to external type definition repositories or package registries
Cannot modify project files directly, only provides code suggestions and configurations

use_cases.title: Enterprise TypeScript Migration
use_cases.description: Guide large-scale JavaScript to TypeScript migration with incremental strategies, tsconfig configuration, and type annotation patterns.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads managing legacy codebase modernization

use_cases.title: Advanced Type System Design
use_cases.description: Design complex generic utilities, branded types for domain modeling, and type-safe APIs using conditional and template literal types.

use_cases.title: Build Performance Optimization
use_cases.description: Diagnose slow type checking, configure incremental builds, and optimize monorepo TypeScript project references.
use_cases.target_user: Developers working with large TypeScript codebases

prompt_templates.title: Basic Type Question
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a TypeScript error: [paste error]. My code is: [paste code]. What does this error mean and how do I fix it?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner seeking help with common TypeScript errors

prompt_templates.title: Type Configuration Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my tsconfig.json for best practices: [paste config]. Are there any settings I should change for a [library/application] project?

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Type Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a type that [describe requirement]. For example, it should [give examples]. Can you design this using TypeScript's type system?
prompt_templates.scenario: Experienced developer needing complex type utilities

prompt_templates.title: Migration Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm migrating a [size] JavaScript codebase to TypeScript. We use [tools/framework]. What's the best incremental approach and what tsconfig settings should I start with?
prompt_templates.scenario: Team lead planning a migration project

output_examples.input: I keep getting 'implicit any' errors in my function parameters
output_examples.output: Enable noImplicitAny in your tsconfig.json. Then add explicit type annotations: function greet(name: string): string { return `Hello, ${name}`; }. For complex cases, use unknown and narrow: function process(input: unknown) { if (typeof input === 'string') { /* input is now string */ } }

output_examples.input: How do I prevent mixing UserId and OrderId strings?
output_examples.output: Use branded types: type Brand<K, T> = K & { readonly __brand: T }; type UserId = Brand<string, 'UserId'>; type OrderId = Brand<string, 'OrderId'>; Create constructors: const createUserId = (id: string): UserId => id as UserId; This prevents passing UserId where OrderId is expected at compile time.

best_practices: Enable strict mode and noUncheckedIndexedAccess in tsconfig.json for maximum type safety
Prefer interface over type for object shapes to get better error messages and declaration merging
Use unknown instead of any for type safety, then narrow with type guards before using the value
anti_patterns: Using any type to bypass errors instead of properly typing the value
Excessive type assertions (as Type) that bypass type checking without justification
Over-engineering types with complex generics when a simpler solution works

faq.question: Should I use interface or type for defining object shapes?
faq.answer: Prefer interface for object shapes. Interfaces provide better error messages, support declaration merging, and can be extended. Use type for unions, primitives, or when you need type features interfaces don't support.

faq.question: How do I fix 'Cannot find module' errors when the file exists?
faq.answer: Check moduleResolution matches your bundler (bundler for Vite/esbuild, node16 for Node.js). Verify baseUrl and paths in tsconfig.json. For monorepos, ensure workspace protocol is configured. Clear caches: rm -rf node_modules/.cache .tsbuildinfo

faq.question: What is the difference between unknown and any?
faq.answer: any disables type checking completely. unknown requires you to narrow the type before use, maintaining type safety. Always prefer unknown when you don't know the type, then use typeof, instanceof, or custom type guards to narrow.

faq.question: How do I migrate from JavaScript to TypeScript incrementally?
faq.answer: Enable allowJs and checkJs in tsconfig.json first. Rename files to .ts gradually, starting with isolated modules. Add types file by file. Enable strict mode features one at a time. Use ts-migrate or AI assistance for bulk type annotations.

faq.question: Why is my TypeScript compilation so slow?
faq.answer: Enable skipLibCheck and incremental in tsconfig.json. Use precise include/exclude patterns. For monorepos, configure project references with composite. Run npx tsc --extendedDiagnostics to identify bottlenecks. Consider switching to esbuild or swc for faster builds.

faq.question: Do TypeScript path mappings work at runtime?
faq.answer: No, TypeScript paths only work at compile time. For runtime, use ts-node with tsconfig-paths, configure Node.js module resolution, or use a bundler that resolves paths. In production, compile first so paths are resolved in output.
